item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The spookiest anomaly of them all is imperialism. Bone-chilling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm also reluctantly downvoting because I honestly am sick of the trope of 'a few dudes secretly control history, including explicitly taking away major accomplishments from real historical people, somehow always including marginalized people'. The Maya Lin part was an especially shitty cut, given the real story; can't any non-white/straight/etc/dude anywhere accomplish anything major without someone giving the credit to a white/straight/etc/dude, even in a piece of fiction? That's technically a political critique — though everything is political — and I feel irritated that the article made me even have to think about this.

The quality of this piece is very high, and I was engaged all the way to the end. The use of that cliche trope just kills it for me. I'm not sure what I would even recommend to fix the issue, since it's at the core of the concept. I'd probably not downvote if it weren't for the specific "taking credit for ideas" cliche, at the very least, though I still wouldn't like the concept.

I just wish people would aim higher with cliche concepts like this than this. You have a great stylistic and technical execution, full of beautiful details. That will be enough for most readers — including those with standards just as high as mine, but with differing perspectives, or who may agree with me but simply loved this anyway — but I want more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was also my reason for downvoting. This was especially salient in the last note. You try to end the article on a note of looming horror by taking credit for actual human suffering and, what? Saying that there will be more of it? It doesn't work. It's too small of a story to support that weight, completely setting aside the distaste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really well written and I enjoyed it, so I voted positively. But your message seems to devalue the actions of good people and horrible people alike. People who did great things did not do thm of their own accord, so they do not matter, and same for negative ones. Other than that, great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interestingly, the Magnusson Devices were designed to look like the Manhattan Project’s Gadget, which is what is pictured, so that was intentional on Valve’s part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp. Sorry to be the first Debbie-Downer. I'd like to begin by saying this is well written. But it hits some thematic points that do not sit well with me. I don't enjoy when articles attempt to explain real historical events with anomalous influence. Especially horrible things.

The Vietnam War didn't happen, or get escalated, by godly or anomalous influence. People did terrible, horrible shit and the Vietnam War was a result.

Political coups on the part of the US didn't happen because some Bernard wanted to end socialism, it came out of fear and abuse of power.

This site is wont to explain away things like that sometimes, and it just doesn't sit well with me at all. I have downvoted based on this distaste of mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really liked the sensible way the level 2 and level 3 clearence documents were diffrentiated. It makes it seem believable that the level 3 documents could be the final ones. Of course, the level 2 documents are clearly witholding information, but I like the way the level 3 ones seem believable. Also, I like the way each documen builds on the previous, but has a twist, and overrides some of the false information in the previous, without completely changing the idea. A lot of SCPs now seem to use different classification levels as a way to have multiple SCPs in one, have a fake one, with no real information, and then have the real one. This, to me, defeats the purpose. To the SCP foundation, the lower classification documents are a way for some personel to get enough information about an anomally safely study or interact with it, while keeping information that could be dangerous out of the wrong hands. Overall, really liked the way the seperation of classification levels was done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, I really hope it wins! It's a very grandiose concept but executed in a very …I don't want to say modestly, but it's not as massive as I feared. Sometimes minimalism is good.

I also like how the three men are just average people and they stay average, as much as they can, given the situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat.

The use of the Manhattan Project as the setup for this is a well done, as it gives the real-life events and historical impact that project ushered into being (namely the invention of atomic weaponry) juxtaposition against a much more devious and far-reaching fictional alternative.

While the "Your SCP is in a Different Castle" bit from the final document is a bit old hat at this point, The characterization you offer for the three men is well done and serves as a great way of bringing the rest of the article to a powerful end.

+1. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This piece is absolutely marvelous. All of it comes together so very well and makes sense. The three men taking their respective stances as the judges of consensus reality speaks clear for their characters. The whole thing is well paced, too.

It should be obvious that I love this. You have easily earned a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed, good catch

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really really like this one. It's got a nice pace, slowly unfolding from what seems like a fairly routine anomaly into a "well, three guys dictate all of history now" situation. The reveal is excellent too, as initially there's really no telling what happened to the scientists. Take all of the praise and a +1!

item.title: Up-vote
item.content:encoded:
I don't have much to say about this. It's a good article. I think you should be really up in the running for 4k. I don't even really have a nitpick. Continue to write good articles.

Ps: Wow you are good at tapping into the psychology of man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gave this SCP a +1. There are a lot of things that I like about this SCP, such as the fact that they made a bomb that sent people to an alternate reality. I'm just curious what would have happened if someone like Hitler went into SCP-4000-2 (assuming that what happened to Myers, Kamen, and Noble applies to other people). Also, I can't help but think of SCP-3001 for SCP-4000-2 for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a note — the url of the page should be "4000conteststerbai", not "scp4000conteststerbai"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember critting this. Deserves an upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this conceptually and it was executed well enough to get a +1 from me.
One critique: You use the word "seemingly" far too much. Not only does it feel non-clinical, but it is very repetitive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its been really awesome watching this evolve and I love the Kate McTiriss proposal-esque notes in here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so wonderfully weird, although I can't get over how much the bomb in the first tab resembles the Magnusson Device. ++1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such an excellent tribute to the legacy of the Manhattan Project. I see parallels between how the three scientists' lives were permanently changed by the events and stories like Louis Slotin's — this part of history seeped into and sometimes damaged every single person involved with the activities at hand, for better or for worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, I was a little disappointed the final document was so "Everything before this was false!" But… it actually wasn't? Left some things out, sure, but everything that happened in the preceding documents is part of the whole. The general concept is unique, the execution is good, I even like the interview.

I dunno, maybe I will just go ahead and upvote now. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really, really good. Three normal dudes thrust into a position of nearly absolute power by complete accident is a really compelling concept, and you really pulled off the "normal dudes" part, I think. You get a great sense of what the three of them are like just from the transcriptions of the botched experiment and the interviews, and while they agree on things in broad terms, the extent to which their opinions differ causing conflicts that are reflected in history is… I'm running out of synonyms for "good". I love this.

A particular thing I'd like to praise is the tone. Obviously the second note isn't what we'd usually call clinical, and I think that really works. Clinical tone on this site is effective, imo, because of the contrast of the impossible and the mundane; it emphasizes that this kind of stuff is normalized within the Foundation. I think that note gets across exactly the same feel in an entirely different way by replacing "clinical" with "conversational", which is something you don't see a lot.

(I'm also excited to see more Archon skips. They're sort of antithetical to the Foundation's whole Thing™ but I think they're really interesting to explore because of that.)

This contest has been great, a lot of things have come out of it that I really enjoyed, but I don't necessarily want to see take the 4000 spot. This is one of only a couple that I'd be unreservedly happy to see win.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I named this! :D
This article fits the theme quite well and is beautifully written . It was a pleasure to help out with this and I'm glad you finally posted!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are there really any villains?

This is my 4k entry. Credit to the following - Roget, TSAT, Nicolini, Yossi, Westrin, Wery, Shaggy, Modulum, Salvagebar, Buckyboy, Stormbreath, FloppyPhoenix, Cabbage

Image credits -

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Thefalloftheberlinwall1989.JPG
